html:
The Movement Disorders Examination

Observe before touching. Use provocative maneuvers. Describe before diagnosing.

Table 1. Structured Movement Examination

EXAM DOMAIN

MANEUVER

ABNORMAL FINDINGS / WHAT TO LOOK FOR

General Observation

Quiet sitting with and without suppression

Abnormal movements at rest; titubation (rhythmic head/trunk bobbing); spontaneous involuntary movements; suppressibility

Cranial Nerves — Gaze

Fixation of gaze; square wave jerks; smooth pursuit

Nystagmus; impaired gaze maintenance; square wave jerks (small involuntary saccades interrupting fixation); breakdown of smooth pursuit with catch-up saccades

Cranial Nerves — Saccades

Large saccades (horizontal and vertical); blink to initiate saccade

Overshoot (hypermetria) or undershoot (hypometria); slow saccades; blink-initiated saccades (oculomotor apraxia — Ataxia-telangiectasia, AOA1/2, Joubert)

Cranial Nerves — Speech

Coordination, quality, pronunciation, pitch modulation, inflection; sustained 'aah' and 'eeh'

Dysrhythmic/ataxic speech; dysarthria (labial, lingual, guttural); scanning speech / abnormal prosody; voice tremor; soft, strangulated, or breathy voice

Cranial Nerves — Tongue

Side-to-side tongue movements

Slow movements; fasciculations; atrophy (LMN); difficulty with alternating movements

Motor — Tone

Passive ROM all limbs at rest and with distraction

Spasticity (velocity-dependent); rigidity (lead-pipe, uniform); dystonia (patterned, action-induced); hypotonia

Motor — Posture

Arms extended with palms up, in, down, and out

Tremor; abnormal posturing; pronator drift; finger posture abnormalities

Motor — Rebound

Examiner briefly forces extended arms downward, then releases

Excessive rebound overshoot = cerebellar dysfunction; reduced rebound = upper motor neuron / spasticity

Motor — RAM

Rapid alternating movements (hand pronation/supination; finger taps)

Dysdiadochokinesia; irregular rhythm; diminishing amplitude; superimposed abnormal movements; slow speed

Motor — Finger-to-Nose / Toe-to-Finger

Standard FTN and TTF

Dysmetria; action tremor throughout movement; intention tremor (increases as approaching target); superimposed chorea or myoclonus

Motor — Writing

Observe grip, posture, and output

Abnormal hand posture; tremor during writing; writer's cramp (focal task-specific dystonia); superimposed involuntary movements

Gait — Observe

Body posture, base, stride, arm swing, heel-toe, path

Trunk flexed/extended; wide/narrow/scissored base; asymmetric stride; reduced arm swing; absent heel strike; meandering path

Gait — Provocative

Tandem, balance on one foot, reverse walking, sideways walking

Balance impairment; abnormal foot posture; persistence or worsening of abnormal movements on provocative gait tasks

Gait — Movement Activation

Observe for movement changes during gait

Increased amplitude/frequency of abnormal movements; emergence of hyperkinetic movements (dystonia, chorea, tics) during walking or running

Describing Abnormal Movements — A Framework

Systematic description often points directly to diagnosis.

Table 2. Movement Description Framework

DIMENSION

QUESTIONS TO ASK / DESCRIBE

Hyper- vs. hypokinetic

Is there excess movement (hyperkinetic: tremor, chorea, myoclonus, tics, dystonia) or reduction/poverty of movement (hypokinetic: Parkinsonism, bradykinesia)?

Frequency

Is it continuous, intermittent, or paroxysmal? How often does it occur per unit time?

Duration

Brief (myoclonus, chorea — milliseconds to seconds); sustained (dystonia — seconds to minutes); rhythmic regular (tremor)

Voluntary vs. involuntary

Can the patient initiate, modify, or stop the movement? Is there a premonitory urge (tics)?

Rest vs. action

Present at rest (resting tremor, severe dystonia/chorea); with posture held (postural tremor); with movement (action/intention tremor, dystonia); task-specific?

Suppressibility

Can the patient suppress it voluntarily? For how long? With what effort? Tics are often suppressible with urge buildup; chorea is typically not.

Change over time

Does it worsen, improve, fluctuate? Diurnal variation (DRD)? Triggered by fever, fasting, stress, exercise, specific tasks?

Premonitory urge

Preceding sensory urge that is relieved by the movement = strongly suggests tics

Family history

Tremor, dystonia, tics, ataxia, Parkinson — critical for all movement disorders

Exam Tips

  • Observe at rest before touching — contact suppresses many movements.
  • Dual task (count backwards, walk and talk): hyperkinetic movements increase; functional movements disappear.
  • Gait is among the best provocative maneuvers for dystonia, chorea, and ataxia.
  • Don't forget face and trunk — cranial dystonia and truncal titubation are commonly missed.
  • Ask about diurnal variation (worse in evening/with fatigue) — key clue for DRD.
  • Video with consent — invaluable for tracking change over time.

Tremor

Classify by when it occurs relative to movement and posture — not by frequency. Resting, postural, and intention tremors have distinct localizations and diagnostic implications.

Table 3. Tremor Classification and Approach

TREMOR TYPE

CHARACTERISTICS

COMMON CAUSES IN CHILDREN

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH

Postural > Resting (action tremor, regular)

Present when limb is held against gravity; absent or reduced at rest; regular rhythm

Familial essential tremor (AD, family history key); enhanced physiological tremor (caffeine, medications, anxiety, metabolic); early dystonic tremor

Family history → TFTs + electrolytes first; if no family history, add MRI brain. Regular postural tremor with family history = likely essential tremor.

Postural > Resting (irregular, jerky)

Irregular, non-rhythmic; may resemble myoclonus

Myoclonic tremor; dystonic tremor (focal, task-specific, directional — 'null point'); cortical myoclonus

Consider myoclonus-dystonia (SGCE); primary dystonia syndromes; EEG if cortical myoclonus suspected; back-averaging EEG for cortical myoclonus

Resting > Postural (resting tremor)

Present at rest; suppressed or reduced with action; 'pill-rolling' pattern in Parkinson

Conversion/functional tremor; drug-induced Parkinsonism (dopamine blockers); juvenile Parkinson (PARK genes, Wilson disease); very rare primary Parkinsonism in childhood

Wilson disease must be excluded in any child with resting tremor: copper, ceruloplasmin, slit-lamp, 24h urine copper. Medication review. MRI brain.

Intention tremor (cerebellar/action)

Increases as limb approaches target; present during movement, worsens terminally; associated with dysmetria

Cerebellar disease (malformation, ataxia syndromes, posterior fossa tumor, MS); spinocerebellar ataxias; mitochondrial disease

MRI brain/posterior fossa; ataxia workup (see Section 3); vitamin E level; metabolic screen

Medication/toxin-induced

History of exposure; often postural or action; may be any pattern

Albuterol, VPA, amiodarone, caffeine, amphetamines, lithium, cyclosporine, stimulants, arsenic, lead, mercury, toluene, alcohol withdrawal

Medication reconciliation first; toxicology screen; heavy metal screen if exposure suspected

Metabolic tremor

Often postural; context of systemic illness

Hepatic encephalopathy (asterixis — actually a negative myoclonus), hyperthyroidism, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia, hypomagnesemia, pheochromocytoma, mitochondrial disease

CMP, TFTs, ammonia, glucose, magnesium, calcium; urine catecholamines if pheochromocytoma suspected

Tremor Pearls

  • Essential tremor: most common movement disorder at all ages. ~50–70% family history (AD). Postural > resting; bilateral UE; worsens with action. Titubation possible.
  • Dystonic tremor: irregular, directional, position-sensitive; worst at null point of dystonic pull. Distinguished from ET by irregularity and co-occurring dystonic posturing.
  • Wilson disease: tremor + any of dysarthria, psychiatric symptoms, liver disease → copper, ceruloplasmin, slit-lamp. Treatable — missing it is not acceptable.
  • Asterixis ≠ tremor. It is negative myoclonus (brief postural lapse). Elicit with arms outstretched, wrists dorsiflexed. Causes: hepatic encephalopathy, uremia, CO2 retention, drugs.

Ataxia

Approach by tempo: acute, recurrent/episodic, or chronic/progressive. Each category has a largely distinct DDx. Distinguish cerebellar (most common), sensory (Romberg +, worse eyes closed), and frontal (wide base, intact cerebellar exam) ataxia.

Acute-Onset Ataxia in a Previously Healthy Child

Table 4. Differential Diagnosis — Acute Ataxia

ETIOLOGY

TYPICAL PRESENTATION / KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURES

Drug / toxin ingestion

Most common cause of acute ataxia in young children (1–4 yo). Nystagmus and encephalopathy common. Urine toxicology screen first. Onset often sudden.

Acute cerebellitis

Acute severe ataxia ± encephalopathy ± cerebellar swelling on MRI; may follow viral URI (1–3 weeks prior). Can be severe with risk of herniation from cerebellar edema. MRI essential.

Basilar migraine

Ataxia with posterior circulation migrainous features: diplopia, vertigo, headache, photophobia, visual phenomena. Family history of migraine often present.

Benign paroxysmal vertigo of childhood

Migrainous variant: brief episodes of vertigo ± ataxia, often with nausea, pallor, fear; normal between episodes. Age 2–8 typically. No loss of consciousness.

Brainstem encephalitis

Cranial nerve dysfunction (diplopia, facial weakness, dysphagia), encephalopathy, seizures, vomiting. Consider autoimmune (anti-GQ1b in Miller-Fisher) and infectious causes.

Paraneoplastic — neuroblastoma (OMA)

Opsoclonus-myoclonus-ataxia syndrome: encephalopathy, irritability, opsoclonus (chaotic multidirectional saccades), distal myoclonus. Neuroblastoma workup mandatory: MIBG scan, urine HVA/VMA, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.

Conversion / functional

Inconsistent exam; features that do not conform to anatomical localization; typically late first/early second decade; often female; improvement with distraction. Rule-IN based on positive exam signs — see FMD callout.

Trauma — hematoma / post-concussive

Antecedent head trauma history. Post-concussive ataxia: acute, usually resolves. Cerebellar/posterior fossa hematoma: headache, progressive symptoms, signs of raised ICP — CT urgently.

Vertebrobasilar occlusion / stroke

Locked-in syndrome pattern: mute, quadriplegia, aware, communicates with eyes. May have initial transient headache, hemiparesis, involuntary movements. MRI/MRA urgently.

Inflammatory — MS, Miller-Fisher GBS, Kawasaki

MS: first demyelinating event, look for other lesions on MRI. Miller-Fisher: CN palsies (ophthalmoplegia) + areflexia + ataxia; anti-GQ1b. Kawasaki: fever + characteristic skin/mucosal findings.

Cerebellar hemorrhage

Signs of raised ICP (headache, vomiting, altered mental status); neck stiffness; CT urgently — can cause rapid herniation.

Recurrent / Episodic Ataxia

Table 5. Differential Diagnosis — Recurrent Ataxia

ETIOLOGY

KEY FEATURES

Episodic Ataxia Type 1 (KCNA1)

AD. Onset 5–7 yo. Attacks 10 min–6 hours. Myokymia (continuous muscle undulation/rippling) between attacks — pathognomonic. Triggered by startle, exercise, fever. Acetazolamide reduces attacks. Commercial gene testing available.

Episodic Ataxia Type 2 (CACNA1A)

AD. Onset late childhood/adolescence. Attacks last hours. Severe inability to maintain posture; vomiting. Interictal nystagmus common. Acetazolamide reduces attacks. Same gene as FHM1 and SCA6 — allelic. Commercial gene testing available.

GLUT1 Deficiency (SLC2A1)

Ataxia/movement disorder episodes provoked by fasting; may have accompanying lethargy, hypotonia, weakness, headache. ± Seizures, acquired microcephaly. CSF glucose <40 mg/dL (or CSF:serum ratio <0.45). Fasting CSF glucose is the key test. Ketogenic diet highly effective.

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Deficiency

Episodes of ataxia, dysarthria ± weakness/lethargy; provoked by infection, stress, or high-carbohydrate meal. Duration hours to weeks. Elevated CSF lactate. Skin fibroblast studies (females may have false negative from mosaicism). Treat with ketogenic diet ± CoQ10, carnitine, vitamins E/C, alpha lipoic acid.

MSUD — Intermittent Form

Irritability, progressive lethargy; attacks provoked by infection, surgery, or protein meal. Onset 5 months–2 years. Maple syrup odor. Plasma amino acids (elevated BCAA). Aminoaciduria. Diet: arginine, benzoate, alpha-ketoacids.

Hartnup Disease (SLC6A19)

Slow development; photosensitive pellagra-like rash; episodic ataxia ± nystagmus ± mood changes/encephalopathy; increased tone and DTRs between episodes. Worsened by stress/infection. Aminoaciduria (neutral amino acids). Treat with niacin.

Migraine-associated ataxia

Ataxia as part of basilar/vestibular migraine. Other migrainous features present. Treat as classic migraine. Diagnosis of exclusion in a migraineur.

Pseudoataxia — epileptic

Sudden onset with associated confusion; no nystagmus; EEG suggestive of ictal or postictal state. Treat underlying epilepsy with AEDs.

Chronic / Progressive Ataxia

Table 6. Differential Diagnosis — Chronic Ataxia by Genetics

AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE

AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT

X-LINKED / OTHER GENETIC

STRUCTURAL / ACQUIRED

  • Friedreich ataxia (FXN — GAA repeat; NOT on WES)
  • Ataxia-telangiectasia (ATM)
  • Ataxia with oculomotor apraxia 1 (APTX)
  • Ataxia with oculomotor apraxia 2 (SETX)
  • Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (TTPA)
  • Abetalipoproteinemia / Bassen-Kornzweig (MTTP)
  • Hypobetalipoproteinemia + areflexia + RP (HARP/PANK2)
  • Ataxia with CNS hypomyelination / VWM (EIF2B genes)
  • GLUT1 deficiency (chronic form)
  • Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency
  • Hartnup disease (SLC6A19)
  • MSUD
  • Multiple carboxylase deficiency
  • Juvenile sulfatide lipidosis / MLD (ARSA)
  • Juvenile GM2 gangliosidosis
  • Niemann-Pick / sea-blue histiocytosis
  • Ramsay Hunt syndrome (MERRF — also mito)
  • Refsum disease (PHYH — elevated phytanic acid)
  • Respiratory chain disorders (Complex I, III, IV)
  • Marinesco-Sjögren syndrome (SIL1)
  • Spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs 1,2,3,6,7,17 — most are trinucleotide repeats NOT on WES)
  • Dentatorubral-pallidoluysian atrophy (DRPLA — repeat expansion)
  • Episodic ataxia type 1 (KCNA1)
  • Episodic ataxia type 2 (CACNA1A)
  • Hereditary spastic ataxias
  • X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ABCD1)
  • Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease (PLP1)
  • Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (mitochondrial)
  • Fragile X tremor/ataxia syndrome (FMR1 premutation — NOT on WES)
  • Congenital cerebellar malformations (hypoplasia, aplasia, Chiari, Dandy-Walker)
  • Basilar impression
  • Cerebellar astrocytoma
  • Medulloblastoma
  • Ependymoma
  • Cerebellar hemangioblastoma (VHL)
  • Supratentorial tumors with cerebellar effects
  • Multiple sclerosis / inflammatory
  • Post-infectious / para-infectious cerebellitis

Repeat Expansion Disorders — NOT Detected on WES/WGS

  • Standard WES and WGS do NOT detect repeat expansions — send disease-specific testing separately.
  • Affected disorders: Friedreich ataxia (FXN GAA repeat), SCAs (>25 types, various repeats), DRPLA, Fragile X tremor-ataxia (FMR1 premutation), myotonic dystrophy (DMPK).
  • A negative WES does NOT exclude Friedreich ataxia or any SCA.
  • Friedreich ataxia: most common hereditary ataxia (1/50,000). Onset <25 yr; progressive ataxia + areflexia + proprioceptive loss + cardiomyopathy + diabetes + scoliosis.

Workup for Cerebellar Dysfunction by Tempo

Table 7. Ataxia Workup by Tempo

TEMPO

INITIAL WORKUP

ADDITIONAL / TARGETED

Acute

CT head (exclude hemorrhage, mass); urine toxicology; CMP. MRI/MRA if concern for stroke or vascular lesion. LP if encephalopathic and negative scan, or if infectious/inflammatory etiology suspected.

If OMA suspected: MIBG scan, urine HVA/VMA, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ± contrast; LP + serum paraneoplastic panels. Anti-GQ1b if Miller-Fisher pattern. Consider autoimmune encephalitis panel.

Recurrent

MRI brain ± MRA ± MRS. Fasting CSF glucose + simultaneous serum glucose (GLUT1). CSF lactate and pyruvate if PDH/mitochondrial suspected.

Additional genetic and metabolic testing guided by clinical features (see recurrent ataxia table). CACNA1A/KCNA1 gene testing for EA2/EA1 if episodic pattern.

Chronic / progressive

MRI brain ± MRA, MRS. Vitamin E level (AVED — underdiagnosed and treatable). LP: CSF glucose (GLUT1), OCB/IgG index (MS/inflammatory), lactate/pyruvate (mitochondrial).

Genetic panel: NOTE — most SCAs, Friedreich ataxia, DRPLA, and Fragile X are REPEAT EXPANSION disorders and are NOT detected on standard WES/WGS. Send disease-specific repeat expansion testing separately. Metabolic screen (amino acids, organic acids, VLCFA, lysosomal enzymes) based on clinical pattern.

Dystonia

Patterned, directional, action-induced posturing from simultaneous agonist/antagonist co-contraction. Affected limbs are typically strong. Sensory trick (geste antagoniste) — touching the body part transiently relieves it — is nearly pathognomonic.

Primary vs. Secondary Dystonia

  • Primary (isolated): dystonia only; normal MRI; genetic cause common (DYT-TOR1A, GCH1/DRD, DYT-THAP1, DYT-GNAL).
  • Secondary (combined): dystonia + other features or known brain injury (CP, HIE, kernicterus, metabolic disease).
  • Bilateral BG signal on MRI → GA1, Wilson, PKAN, NPC, CO toxicity, mitochondrial (Leigh), post-anoxic.
  • DRD (GCH1): diurnal variation is the hallmark; normal MRI; AD (family history may be negative). Levodopa response often dramatic within days. Trial is mandatory.

Treatment Goal

Reduce dystonia enough to improve function without causing worsened secretions, dysphagia, weakness, or sedation. Multimodal; benefit may take weeks to months.

Trihexyphenidyl (Artane) Titration

Titrate slowly — rushing causes anticholinergic toxicity. Allow 2–4 weeks at each level before assessing benefit.

Full Pharmacological Reference — Dystonia Medications

Table 10. Oral Medications for Dystonia — Dosing and Adverse Effects

MEDICATION / MOA

STARTING DOSE

TITRATION

GOAL DOSE

ADVERSE EFFECTS

KEY NOTES

FIRST-LINE AGENTS

Trihexyphenidyl (Artane) MOA: Anticholinergic

0.05 mg/kg/day daily–BID

Inc by 0.05–0.3 mg/kg/day weekly; titrate to TID over 5–6 weeks (see standard titration schedule)

0.05–0.5 mg/kg/day div TID (max 0.75 mg/kg/day; up to 2.6 mg/kg/day reported) Tabs: 2 mg, 5 mg | Soln: 0.4 mg/mL

Central: dizziness, N/V, behavioral/cognitive changes, anxiety, chorea, insomnia, nightmares Peripheral: constipation, urinary retention, dry mouth, blurred vision

CI: narrow-angle glaucoma. Paradoxical effects at low doses. 2–4 weeks to full benefit. Reduce dose if cognition declines.

Carbidopa/Levodopa (Sinemet) MOA: Dopamine precursor

1 mg/kg/day (levodopa component); start AM/noon doses

Inc by 1 mg/kg/day weekly

4–5 mg/kg/day div TID (6–10 mg/kg/day in AR-DRD; up to 16–20 mg/kg/day reported) Tabs IR: 25/100 mg

GI upset, nausea, orthostatic hypotension, fatigue, insomnia. Rare with dystonia dosing: constipation, dyskinesias, hallucinations

MANDATORY trial in dystonia + normal MRI. Avoid abrupt DC. Give last dose ≤1600 to reduce insomnia. Add carbidopa (Lodosyn) 12.5–25 mg TID for persistent nausea. Separate from iron by ≥2h.

ADJUNCTIVE AGENTS

Baclofen (Lioresal) MOA: GABA-B agonist

<2 yr: 2.5 mg qHS 2–7 yr: 2.5–5 mg qHS ≥8 yr: 5 mg qHS

Inc 2.5–5 mg/day q3 days–weekly

<2 yr: max 40 mg/day 2–7 yr: max 60 mg/day ≥8 yr: max 200 mg/day Tabs: 5, 10, 20 mg | Soln: 1, 5 mg/mL

Sedation, dizziness, nausea, fatigue, trunk/head weakness, worsened swallowing

Avoid abrupt DC — withdrawal risk (seizures, autonomic instability). Works better for spasticity than dystonia; useful when mixed picture.

Clonazepam (Klonopin) MOA: GABA-A positive modulator

<30 kg: 0.01–0.03 mg/kg/day div TID-QID ≥30 kg: ≤0.25 mg/dose TID

<30 kg: inc 0.01–0.03 mg/kg/day q3–5 days ≥30 kg: inc 0.5–1 mg/day q3–5 days

0.05–0.2 mg/kg/day div TID Tabs: 0.5, 1, 2 mg | ODT available

Sedation, depression, nocturnal drooling, behavioral disinhibition

Avoid abrupt DC. Useful adjunct at night for dystonic spasms.

Diazepam (Valium) MOA: GABA-A positive modulator

0.1–0.3 mg/kg/day div BID-QID (max 10 mg/dose)

Inc 0.1 mg/kg/day weekly

0.8 mg/kg/day div BID-QID >12 yr: 2–10 mg/dose BID-QID Tabs: 2, 5, 10 mg | Soln: 5 mg/mL

Sedation, depression, nocturnal drooling, behavioral disinhibition

Avoid abrupt DC. Useful for acute dystonic spasm and comfort management.

Gabapentin (Neurontin) MOA: α2-δ subunit voltage-gated Ca channel blocker

10–15 mg/kg/day div TID

Titrate to effective dose based on tolerability

Up to 60 mg/kg/day div TID (mean reported in dystonia: 18 mg/kg/dose TID) Tabs: 300, 600, 800 mg | Soln: 250 mg/5 mL

Sedation, emotional lability, nystagmus, tremor, ataxia, peripheral edema

Dose adjust in renal impairment. Higher doses may be needed for dystonia than for epilepsy/neuropathic pain.

Tetrabenazine (Xenazine) MOA: Reversible VMAT2 inhibitor

3.125–12.5 mg/day (AM)

Inc 3.125–12.5 mg/day weekly, increasing to TID dosing

37.5–50 mg div TID (up to 200 mg/day reported) Tabs: 12.5, 25 mg

Sedation, Parkinsonism, depression, akathisia, nervousness, insomnia, suicidal ideation

CYP2D6 genotyping recommended for doses >50 mg/day.

Deutetrabenazine (Austedo) MOA: VMAT2 inhibitor

6 mg daily (AM)

Inc 6 mg/day weekly to effect

Doses >12 mg: divide BID Max: 48 mg/day Tabs: 6, 9, 12 mg

Sedation, Parkinsonism, depression, akathisia, nervousness, insomnia

Brand only. Baseline EKG; repeat after dose >24 mg/day. Consider CYP2D6 genotyping.

Oxcarbazepine (Trileptal) MOA: Na channel blocker

5 mg/kg/day daily (week 1)

Inc 5 mg/kg/day weekly to effect (div BID)

5–20 mg/kg/day div BID Susp: 60 mg/mL | Tabs: 150, 300, 600 mg

Sedation, N/V, hyponatremia, rash; ataxia and diplopia at high levels

Generally patients respond to lower doses than with epilepsy. Weak CYP inducer (may decrease OCPs).

Clonidine (Catapres) MOA: α2-adrenergic agonist

1–2 mcg/kg/dose qHS–TID

Inc 1–2 mcg/kg/day q3–5 days

2–4 mcg/kg/dose TID-QID (up to 20 mcg/kg/day div q4–6h reported) Tabs: 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 mg | TTS patch available

Sedation, bradycardia, hypotension

Avoid abrupt DC — rebound HTN. Patch useful for compliance.

Tizanidine (Zanaflex) MOA: α2-adrenergic agonist

≥2–10 yr: 1 mg qHS ≥10 yr: 2 mg qHS

Titrate to effective dose based on tolerability

0.3–0.5 mg/kg/day div TID-QID Max: 24 mg/day Tabs/Caps: 2, 4, 6 mg

Sedation, hypotension, dizziness, dry mouth

Avoid abrupt DC. Dose adjust in hepatic impairment. Monitor LFTs.

Dantrolene (Dantrium) MOA: Skeletal muscle relaxant (ryanodine receptor)

≥5 yr: 0.5 mg/kg/day qHS–BID

Inc 0.5–1 mg/kg/day weekly

12 mg/kg/day div TID-QID Max: 400 mg/day Caps: 25, 50, 100 mg

Sedation, dizziness, fatigue, diarrhea

Can mix capsule contents with juice/water. Hepatotoxicity risk — monitor LFTs.

Procedural and Surgical Options

  • BoNT-A: focal chemodenervation; repeat q3–6 months. Effective for focal dystonia (cervical, limb, writer's cramp). Use with PT and casting. Ultrasound/EMG guidance improves accuracy.
  • Phenol: stronger than BoNT; large LE muscle groups; requires anesthesia; longer duration; can combine with BoNT.
  • ITB: better for spasticity than dystonia — unpredictable for pure dystonia. Useful in mixed picture. Consider test dose before pump implant.
  • DBS (GPi): best for mobile dystonia without fixed contractures. Primary dystonia (DYT-TOR1A, DYT-THAP1) responds best. Secondary dystonia response variable. Programming takes weeks to months.

Chorea

Rapid, irregular, non-patterned, flowing movements. No premonitory urge; not suppressible; worst distally at rest; worsens with voluntary movement. Hardest to distinguish from cortical myoclonus.

Differential Diagnosis — Acute / Subacute Chorea

Table 11. Acute/Subacute Chorea — Differential Diagnosis

CATEGORY / ETIOLOGY

EXAMPLES / KEY FEATURES

Autoimmune

Sydenham chorea (post-streptococcal — most common acquired chorea in children); SLE; antiphospholipid antibody syndrome; Henoch-Schönlein Purpura

Infectious / encephalitis

Mycoplasma; Lyme; HSV; TB; encephalitis (consider autoimmune encephalitis panel)

Vascular / hypoxic-ischemic

Stroke (including moyamoya); vasculitis; 'post-pump chorea' after cardiac bypass

Metabolic

MELAS; acute basal ganglia injury (metabolic crisis); mitochondrial disorders

Endocrine / electrolyte

Thyroid dysfunction (hyper- and hypo-); hypo/hyperparathyroidism; hypocalcemia; hypomagnesemia; hypo/hypernatremia; chorea gravidarum

Choreic CP / exacerbated dyskinesia

Baseline dyskinetic CP with worsening during febrile illness — ask about perinatal history

Paroxysmal / episodic

Paroxysmal kinesiogenic dyskinesia (PKD — triggered by sudden movement; PRRT2); paroxysmal non-kinesiogenic dyskinesia (PNKD — triggered by caffeine, alcohol, fatigue; MR1); paroxysmal exercise-induced chorea/dyskinesia (PED — exercise-triggered; GLUT1)

Iatrogenic — dopamine receptor blockers

Acute tardive; withdrawal-emergent chorea; neuroleptics/antipsychotics; antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)

Drug-induced (see full table below)

Stimulants (amphetamines, methylphenidate); antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine, VPA, phenobarbital); calcium channel blockers; TCAs; lithium; L-dopa; clonidine; amantadine; OCPs; benzodiazepines

Complex migraine / alternating hemiplegia

Migraine with motor features; alternating hemiplegia of childhood (ATP1A3)

Drug-Induced Chorea — Reference List

Table 12. Drug-Induced Chorea — Agents by Class

DRUG CLASS

AGENTS

Stimulants / amphetamines

Amphetamine/dextroamphetamine (Adderall), dexmethylphenidate (Focalin), dextroamphetamine (Dexedrine), methamphetamine, methylphenidate (Ritalin), atomoxetine (Strattera)

Anticholinergics

Trihexyphenidyl (Artane), benztropine (Cogentin), diphenhydramine (Benadryl)

Antiemetics

Chorea Workup

Initial Chorea Workup (All Cases)

  • ASO titer / anti-DNAse B — Sydenham chorea (post-streptococcal)
  • CRP / ESR — inflammatory
  • ANA / antiphospholipid antibodies — SLE, APLA syndrome
  • TFTs — thyroid dysfunction
  • CBC, CMP — baseline; glucose; electrolytes
  • Serum ceruloplasmin and copper — Wilson disease (always in new-onset chorea)
  • Brain MRI — structural, basal ganglia signal, WM
  • Additional infectious testing based on clinical context: HSV, Lyme, HIV, WNV, Mycoplasma, Legionella

Treatment of Chorea

Anti-Chorea Treatment

  • Benzodiazepines (diazepam, clonazepam): first-line for acute control.
  • Levetiracetam: evidence for Sydenham and acquired chorea; well tolerated.
  • Valproic acid: effective; monitor LFTs, CBC, platelets.
  • Dopamine blockers (risperidone, haloperidol): severely disabling chorea; tardive dyskinesia risk with long-term use.
  • Tetrabenazine / deutetrabenazine: VMAT2 inhibitors; good for chronic severe chorea; no tardive risk.
  • DO NOT use anticholinergics — they worsen chorea.
  • Sydenham: Echo + EKG; penicillin prophylaxis until age 40; prednisone 2 mg/kg/day x 4 wks (superior to placebo); IVIG or PLEX if refractory or steroids contraindicated.

Functional Tic-Like Movements (FTLMs)

  • Surged dramatically post-2020; adolescent females; social media contagion. NOT rare.
  • Clues: sudden full onset (days); large proximal arm/body movements at onset; coprolalia/copropraxia early; no waxing-waning; no caudal spread; minimal premonitory urge; worsen with attention.
  • Rule-IN diagnosis based on positive exam — not a diagnosis of exclusion. See FMD section.

DDx for Apparent Tics

  • Akathisia — inner restlessness; no suppressibility with urge
  • Chorea — non-suppressible, flowing, no urge
  • Compulsions — ego-dystonic, obsession-driven
  • Dystonia — sustained, patterned, action-induced
  • Myoclonus — shock-like, brief, no urge
  • Paroxysmal kinesiogenic dyskinesia — triggered by sudden movement
  • Functional movements — see FMD section
  • Stereotypies — rhythmic, fixed, voluntary; ± ASD
  • Tardive dyskinesia — orofacial, dopamine blocker history, not suppressible

Non-Pharmacological Tic Treatment

Non-Pharmacological Treatment — Try First

  • Education: patient, family, school. Tics are neurological, not willful.
  • CBIT (Comprehensive Behavioral Intervention for Tics): first-line; evidence-based; refer to trained psychologist.
  • Tic breaks: scheduled intentional release to reduce suppression burden.
  • Stress reduction: relaxation, measured breathing, visualization.
  • Treat comorbidities first — ADHD and OCD treatment often reduces tics significantly.
  • Supplements with some evidence: magnesium, riboflavin, CoQ10. Reduce sugar and caffeine.

Reserve medications for functionally impairing or distressing tics. Treat comorbidities first.

Functional Movement Disorders (FMD)

Common — up to 15–20% of new movement disorder clinic referrals. Rule-IN diagnosis based on positive exam findings, not a diagnosis of exclusion.

FMD — Conceptual Framework

  • Mechanism: disorder of movement control and attention; abnormal predictive coding. Not simulation or malingering.
  • Avoid 'psychogenic' or 'hysterical' — stigmatizing and inaccurate. Psychosocial stressors common but not required.
  • FTLMs surged since 2020 — social media contagion is a real mechanism. Recognize it.
  • The disorder is real. Diagnosis gives a treatable pathway — frame it positively.

Positive Exam Signs (Rule-In Features)

  • Distractibility: movement diminishes or disappears with attention directed elsewhere — most reliable sign.
  • Entrainment: functional tremor entrains to voluntary contralateral rhythmic movement at different frequency.
  • Variability: movement changes character, distribution, or amplitude inconsistently during exam.
  • Inconsistency: findings don't follow anatomical localization of any recognized neurological syndrome.
  • Hoover sign: hip extension weakness reverses with synergistic contralateral hip flexion against resistance.
  • Coactivation sign (functional tremor): rigidity synchronizes with voluntary contralateral tremor — not seen in structural tremor.
  • Positive response to neutral suggestion or PT trial supports FMD.

Communicating the Diagnosis and Treatment

  • Lead with the rule-in: 'Your exam shows specific features I recognize as functional — I'm not diagnosing this by exclusion.'
  • Avoid premature closure — FMD and structural disease can coexist.
  • Treatment: FMD-specific physiotherapy is most evidence-based. CBT for anxiety/OCD. MDT (neurology, PT, psychology).
  • Avoid excessive testing and polypharmacy — reinforces illness behavior.
  • Early confident diagnosis improves prognosis. Delayed diagnosis and prolonged workup worsen outcomes.

Cerebral Palsy

Definition and Conceptual Framework

The current consensus definition of CP (Rosenbaum et al., 2007) is: "Cerebral palsy describes a group of permanent disorders of the development of movement and posture, causing activity limitation, that are attributed to non-progressive disturbances that occurred in the developing fetal or infant brain." The motor disorders of CP are often accompanied by disturbances of sensation, perception, cognition, communication, and behaviour, by epilepsy, and by secondary musculoskeletal problems.

CP Is a Clinical Description, Not an Etiological Diagnosis — This Matters

  • The 2007 definition deliberately frames CP as a clinical descriptor, not a disease entity with a single cause. This was a paradigm shift from earlier frameworks that treated CP as a unified diagnosis.
  • Receiving a CP label should always prompt the question: WHY does this child have CP? The etiology is not established by the clinical diagnosis alone.
  • Etiological workup is not optional. MRI, and in many cases genetic testing, is part of the standard of care evaluation for CP — not something done only when the diagnosis is in doubt.
  • The clinical utility of establishing an etiology: (1) recurrence risk counseling; (2) identification of treatable mimickers; (3) prognosis refinement; (4) targeted therapy (e.g., mTOR inhibitors in TSC-related spasticity); (5) access to condition-specific resources and research.
  • A normal MRI in a child with apparent CP is not reassuring — it is a red flag that should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis and formal neurogenetic evaluation.

Key Definitional Features

Non-progressive: The brain injury is static. Motor function may improve with development, therapy, and intervention, and may worsen with aging, musculoskeletal complications, or fatigue — but the underlying neurological lesion does not progress. Any child with CP who is losing motor skills must be re-evaluated for a progressive disorder.

Developing brain: The injury occurs in the fetal or infant brain (by convention, up to age 2–3 years). Acquired brain injuries after this window produce neurological deficits but are not classified as CP.

Etiological agnosticism: The definition does not specify a cause. Prematurity, HIE, stroke, infection, malformation, and genetic disorders can all produce CP. The relative contribution of genetic factors to CP is increasingly recognized — up to 30% of CP cases may have a contributing genetic variant identified with modern genomic tools.

Classification: Subtypes and Functional Severity

Motor Subtypes

CP is classified by the predominant motor tone/movement abnormality and topographic distribution. Correct subtype identification is clinically important because it guides treatment selection — particularly distinguishing spasticity from dystonia.

Spastic CP (~80% of CP)

Diplegia (LE > UE); hemiplegia (unilateral); quadriplegia/bilateral

Spasticity (velocity-dependent increased tone); hyperreflexia; clonus; Babinski; scissoring gait (diplegia); circumduction (hemiplegia)

Diplegia: periventricular WM injury (PVL) — preterm. Hemiplegia: focal cortical/subcortical lesion, MCA infarct, periventricular injury. Quadriplegia: diffuse cortical injury, HIE central pattern, malformation.

Diplegia most common in prematurity — PVL disrupts periventricular fibers to LE. Hemiplegia: ~50% term, ~50% preterm; important to image to exclude structural cause or mimicker. Quadriplegia: worst functional prognosis; highest comorbidity burden.

Dyskinetic CP (~15% of CP)

Predominantly truncal and limb; often whole-body

Dystonia (sustained/patterned twisting, often action-induced) ± choreoathetosis (involuntary writhing/flowing). Tone fluctuates. May coexist with spasticity.

Bilateral basal ganglia and/or thalamic signal change: term HIE (central/profound pattern → putaminal + VPL thalamic injury). Kernicterus → globus pallidus T1-bright (neonatal), later T2-bright; auditory neuropathy.

Pure dystonia CP: always consider dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD/GCH1) — a treatable mimic. Levodopa trial is warranted. Kernicterus triad: dystonia + hearing loss (auditory neuropathy spectrum) + upward gaze palsy. Ask about neonatal hyperbilirubinemia.

Ataxic CP (~5% of CP)

Predominantly truncal and appendicular

Cerebellar ataxia; hypotonia; dysmetria; intention tremor; dysdiadochokinesia. Tone low rather than increased.

Cerebellar hypoplasia/atrophy; posterior fossa malformation; diffuse WM injury

Ataxic CP is a diagnosis of exclusion — cerebellar ataxia in a child warrants thorough consideration of progressive ataxias (ATXN, ATM, POLG, SCA types) before labeling as CP. MRI and genetic workup are essential. Non-progressive course required for CP diagnosis.

Mixed CP

Variable

Features of more than one subtype — most commonly spasticity + dystonia

Variable; reflects mixed mechanisms of injury

Spasticity and dystonia co-occur frequently — distinguishing them on exam matters because treatment differs. Spasticity: velocity-dependent, clasp-knife; dystonia: patterned, action-induced, posture-dependent. Incorrect identification leads to wrong treatment selection.

Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS)

The GMFCS is the standard functional classification system for CP motor severity, described by Palisano et al. (1997) and validated across populations. It classifies children into five levels based on self-initiated movement with emphasis on sitting, transfers, and mobility. GMFCS level should be documented at every clinic visit — it drives treatment decisions, equipment prescription, surveillance protocols (particularly hip X-ray surveillance), and prognosis communication. GMFCS is age-specific (different descriptors for <2 years, 2–4, 4–6, 6–12, 12–18 years).

Gait aids (walker, crutches) often helpful outdoors. Fatigue is significant. Falls risk. Physiotherapy focus on endurance and fall prevention.

MACS — Manual Ability Classification System

  • The MACS classifies how children with CP use their hands to handle objects in daily activities — the upper extremity analog of GMFCS.
  • Five levels: I (handles objects easily) → V (does not handle objects; severely limited).
  • Document MACS in children with UE involvement. It is distinct from GMFCS and provides complementary functional information.
  • CFCS (Communication Function Classification System) is also available for children with communication impairment — worth knowing exists.

Clinical Examination in CP

The neurological examination in CP requires deliberate attention to tone quality, reflex pattern, movement character, and functional implications. The most common resident error is performing a standard adult-style neurological exam and missing the nuanced tone and movement findings that distinguish CP subtypes and guide management.

Table 3. Examination Findings in CP — Key Domains, Findings, and Tips

EXAM DOMAIN

FINDINGS IN CP

EXAM TIPS / PITFALLS

Tone assessment

Spasticity: velocity-dependent resistance; clasp-knife catch at end-range; more pronounced with fast passive movement. Dystonia: sustained patterned posturing; often action-induced or triggered by intent; may appear normal at rest. Rigidity: uniform lead-pipe resistance throughout ROM (less common in CP; think extrapyramidal disorders if prominent).

Always assess tone at REST and with MOVEMENT. Spasticity is absent at rest — assess during fast passive limb movement. Dystonia may be absent at rest and only emerge with voluntary action or startle. Asking child to walk, reach, or perform dual tasks unmasks movement-dependent tone changes that are invisible at rest.

Reflexes

Hyperreflexia; clonus (sustained or unsustained); Babinski (extensor plantar) and other pathological reflexes; spread of reflexes; jaw jerk (supratentorial bilateral disease).

Clonus: distinguish sustained (≥3 beats) from unsustained — sustained clonus more reliably pathological. Asymmetric hyperreflexia in an infant is always abnormal and warrants MRI. Hyporeflexia in apparent 'CP' should raise concern for a peripheral process or mimicker.

Primitive reflexes

Persistence of primitive reflexes beyond expected age: ATNR (asymmetric tonic neck reflex — fencer posture, persists >6 months), Moro (persists >6 months), STNR (symmetric tonic neck reflex), tonic labyrinthine reflex.

Persistent ATNR beyond 6 months interferes with midline hand use and rolling. Obligate primitive reflexes (child cannot break out of reflex posture) are more clinically significant than non-obligate. Note whether reflexes interfere with function.

Gait and motor patterns

Diplegic: scissoring (hip adductor spasticity), toe-walking (equinus), crouched gait, internal rotation. Hemiplegic: circumduction, equinovarus foot, arm flexion posturing. Dyskinetic: fluctuating, twisting, overflow movements worsened by intention.

Observe gait in both directions, on toes, heels, tandem. Ask child to run — abnormal patterns often more apparent. Unilateral toe-walking requires MRI to exclude focal hemispheric lesion. Document which features are functional limitations vs. merely present.

Upper limb function

Hemiplegia: fisting, thumb-in-palm, wrist flexion, pronation posturing; cortical hand signs (fine motor > gross motor disparity). Diplegia: usually preserved UE function. Quadriplegia: variable bilateral UE involvement.

The MACS (Manual Ability Classification System) is the UE equivalent of GMFCS — know it exists. Bimanual CP: in hemiplegia, examine for hand preference before 18 months, which is always abnormal and should prompt MRI.

Comorbidities to assess at every visit

Cognitive/intellectual disability (~50% of CP overall; higher in quadriplegia). Epilepsy (~35–50%). Communication impairment. Vision (strabismus, CVI, refractive error). Hearing. Feeding/swallowing dysfunction (GERD, aspiration). Bladder dysfunction. Pain. Mental health (anxiety, depression).

CVI (cortical/cerebral visual impairment) is the most common cause of childhood visual impairment in high-income countries and is under-recognized in CP. Features: better peripheral than central vision, visual fatigue, crowding phenomenon, preference for familiar objects. Formal CVI assessment by functional vision specialist, not standard acuity chart.

Hip surveillance

Hip displacement/dislocation occurs in up to 35% of children with CP overall; approaches 90% at GMFCS IV–V. Painless early, painful late — windswept deformity, pelvic obliquity.

Hip surveillance X-ray (migration percentage) is standard of care for all children with CP: every 6 months for GMFCS III–V until skeletal maturity; annually for GMFCS II. Do NOT rely on clinical exam alone — early hip displacement is clinically silent. Missed hip dislocation is a source of chronic pain and functional loss.

Etiological Workup

Brain MRI identifies a cause in approximately 80% of CP cases overall. The remaining ~20% — particularly those with normal MRI — have a significantly higher yield for genetic diagnoses and mimickers. Etiological workup is not a secondary concern; it is part of the standard evaluation of every child with CP.

Table 4. Etiological Workup by Clinical Scenario

CLINICAL SCENARIO

RECOMMENDED WORKUP

RATIONALE / NOTES

All children with CP

Brain MRI (if not already done). Consider repeat if initial was in neonatal period or quality suboptimal.

MRI identifies etiology in ~80% of CP cases. A normal MRI in CP warrants serious consideration of mimickers and genetic workup — normal MRI + CP phenotype is not a reassuring combination, it is a diagnostic red flag.

Normal MRI or MRI not explaining clinical phenotype

Chromosomal microarray. Epilepsy gene panel or WES/WGS (especially if treatment-refractory epilepsy or regression). Metabolic screen (plasma amino acids, acylcarnitines, urine organic acids, lactate) if not previously done.

A normal MRI in apparent CP has a meaningful genetic yield. In one systematic review, ~14% of children with CP had a genetic cause identified — many with normal or non-specific MRI findings.

Dyskinetic/dystonic CP with normal MRI

Levodopa trial (mandatory). CSF neurotransmitters (HVA, 5-HIAA, pterins, 5-MTHF) if levodopa response incomplete. GCH1/TH gene testing. Plasma amino acids (arginine for ARG1 deficiency).

DRD is the single most important treatable mimic of dyskinetic CP — it must be excluded with a levodopa trial in every child with dystonia and a normal MRI before a diagnosis of dyskinetic CP is considered secure.

Family history of similar disorder; consanguinity; dysmorphic features

Chromosomal microarray first; WES/WGS if microarray unrevealing. Consider targeted gene panels based on phenotype.

Positive family history, parental consanguinity, or dysmorphic features substantially raise the prior probability of a genetic diagnosis. These children warrant comprehensive genetic evaluation even if MRI is abnormal.

Progressive course or developmental regression

Stop and reconsider the CP diagnosis. Initiate metabolic screen + WES/WGS. Refer to neurogenetics.

CP by definition is non-progressive. Any child with apparent CP who is losing skills is NOT CP until proven otherwise.

CP Mimickers — When to Reconsider the Diagnosis

CP is a clinical diagnosis of inclusion — but it should only be made after treatable and progressive disorders have been considered. The following red flags should prompt reconsideration:

Red Flags That Should Make You Reconsider a CP Diagnosis

  • Progressive course: loss of motor milestones or worsening neurological function. CP does not progress — if it appears to, it is not CP.
  • Normal MRI: a normal MRI in apparent CP is a red flag, not reassurance. Genetic and metabolic evaluation is warranted.
  • Diurnal variation in tone or movement disorder: strongly suggests DRD — levodopa trial is mandatory.
  • Prominent dystonia + normal MRI: DRD, organic acidemias (GA1, ARG1), mitochondrial disease, NKH, Wilson disease.
  • Family history of similar disorder or consanguinity: raises prior probability of genetic etiology substantially.
  • Neonatal period was reportedly normal and symptom onset was later: CP typically has identifiable perinatal context.
  • Regression at any point: metabolic or degenerative disorder until proven otherwise.
  • Unexpected comorbidity pattern: vision loss out of proportion to motor findings, neuropathy, organomegaly — suggest systemic disorder.
  • Failure to follow expected natural history: GMFCS level worsening significantly, unexpected loss of hand function.

Many leukodystrophies present in infancy/early childhood with spasticity and can mimic spastic CP before the progressive course becomes apparent. Serial MRI is critical. X-ALD, MLD, Krabbe all have initial presentations that can be mistaken for CP.

MRI with careful WM evaluation; metabolic screen; leukodystrophy gene panel (see Neurometabolic chapter)

Rett syndrome (MECP2)

Genetic / neurodevelopmental

Regression after period of normal development (12–18 months); hand stereotypies replacing purposeful hand use; loss of language; breathing irregularities; acquired microcephaly; almost exclusively females

Classic Rett is not a CP mimic in the traditional sense, but early-stage Rett with hypotonia and regression is sometimes initially considered CP. Hand stereotypies and breathing dysregulation distinguish it.

MECP2 sequencing (females); CDKL5 (atypical/early-onset variant); FOXG1 (congenital variant)

Arginase deficiency (ARG1)

Treatable metabolic (UCD)

Progressive spastic diplegia/tetraplegia with onset in childhood; hyperammonemia (may be mild/intermittent); elevated plasma arginine; no clear perinatal injury

Urea cycle defect causing progressive spasticity — not encephalopathy as in other UCDs. Often misdiagnosed as CP for years. Plasma arginine markedly elevated. Treatable with dietary protein restriction + nitrogen scavengers.

Plasma amino acids (elevated arginine); urine organic acids (orotic acid); ARG1 gene; serum ammonia

Glutaric aciduria type 1 (GCDH)

Treatable metabolic (organic acidemia)

Macrocephaly; bilateral striatal injury (caudate/putamen) after febrile encephalopathic crisis in infancy; dyskinetic/dystonic movement disorder; characteristic MRI (frontotemporal hypoplasia + striatal signal)

Macrocephaly + dyskinesia following acute encephalopathic crisis in infancy = GA1 until proven otherwise. MRI: frontotemporal volume loss + bilateral striatal lesions. Treatable with lysine-restricted diet + carnitine if caught pre-crisis (NBS in KY).

Urine organic acids (glutarylcarnitine); acylcarnitine profile (C5-DC); GCDH gene; NBS (captured in KY)

Niemann-Pick C (NPC1/NPC2)

Metabolic / lysosomal

Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy; progressive ataxia + dystonia; cognitive regression; hepatosplenomegaly; gelastic cataplexy

VSGP is the clinical hallmark — test upgaze, downgaze, and lateral saccades in any child with unexplained movement disorder + cognitive decline. NPC is slowly progressive, not static.

Plasma oxysterols (7-ketocholesterol); NPC1/NPC2 gene; filipin staining if needed

Mitochondrial disease (various)

Genetic / metabolic

Episodic decompensation; elevated lactate; multi-organ involvement; regression; Leigh syndrome pattern on MRI (symmetric basal ganglia/brainstem T2 signal)

Mitochondrial disease can produce spastic, ataxic, or dystonic phenotypes. Progressive course, multi-organ involvement, and MRI pattern distinguish from CP. MELAS: stroke-like episodes, not conforming to vascular territory.

Serum/CSF lactate; acylcarnitines; mitochondrial gene panel/WES; MRI (Leigh pattern)

Spinal cord abnormality (tethered cord, tumor, syrinx)

Structural

Progressive spastic diplegia; bowel/bladder dysfunction; back pain; cutaneous spinal stigmata (sacral dimple, hair tuft, hemangioma); scoliosis

Spinal cord pathology can present identically to spastic diplegia — the brain MRI may be normal. Tethered cord, spinal cord tumor, cervical myelopathy from instability (Down syndrome, skeletal dysplasia) are important to exclude.

Spine MRI (full spine); neurosurgical referral if tethered cord or compressive lesion identified

Treatment of Spasticity and Dystonia

Before treating tone, identify what type of tone abnormality is present. Spasticity and dystonia require different treatments — treating dystonia with an antispasticity agent alone is ineffective, and selecting SDR for a child with significant dystonia produces poor outcomes. Many children with CP have mixed tone (spasticity + dystonia), which requires a combined approach.

Treatment goals must be individualized: for ambulatory children (GMFCS I–III), the goal is optimizing function and gait. For non-ambulatory children (GMFCS IV–V), the goals shift toward comfort, ease of care, pain reduction, positioning, and prevention of hip dislocation and scoliosis. These are different goals requiring different treatment approaches.

ORAL AGENTS

Baclofen (oral)

Spasticity (spinal > supraspinal)

Start 2.5–5 mg BID-TID; titrate slowly. Max ~60–80 mg/day (children). Weight-based in young children.

First-line oral antispasticity agent. Effective for lower extremity spasticity (diplegia). Useful for spasms and clonus. Has limited CNS penetration — intrathecal (ITB) achieves far higher spinal concentrations.

Sedation (most common — dose-limiting); weakness; ABRUPT DISCONTINUATION → withdrawal (seizures, hallucinations, autonomic instability — potentially fatal). Taper slowly.

0.04–0.08 mg/kg/dose TID-QID; max ~0.8 mg/kg/day

Useful for acute spasm, painful spasticity, and mixed spasticity-dystonia. Tolerance develops with chronic use. Valuable for short-term management and procedural/comfort use in severe CP.

Sedation; tolerance; dependence; respiratory depression (caution with sleep-disordered breathing). Paradoxical agitation in some children.

Tizanidine

Spasticity

Start 0.05 mg/kg/dose at night; titrate to TID. Off-label in children.

Alternative to baclofen; may be better tolerated in some. Hepatotoxicity risk — monitor LFTs at baseline and periodically. Less evidence in pediatric CP than baclofen.

Sedation; hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs); hypotension; dry mouth. Drug interactions (CYP1A2 inhibitors increase levels significantly).

AGENTS FOR DYSTONIA

Trihexyphenidyl (Artane)

Muscarinic anticholinergic → reduces thalamocortical and striatal cholinergic drive

Dystonia (primary and secondary)

Start 0.5–1 mg/day; titrate slowly over months to effect. Children often tolerate higher doses (mg/kg) than adults. Target 12–20 mg/day in older children.

Most established oral agent for childhood dystonia, including dyskinetic CP. Requires slow, gradual titration — rush titration → anticholinergic toxicity. More effective in younger children and primary dystonias than secondary (CP).

Anticholinergic: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, cognitive impairment (dose-limiting), behavioral change. Reduce dose if cognition declines.

Levodopa / carbidopa

Dopamine precursor → restores dopaminergic tone in striatum

Dystonia — especially DRD; partial benefit in some dyskinetic CP

Start 1–2 mg/kg/day levodopa component divided TID. DRD: dramatic response to very low doses (1–2 mg/kg/day). Dyskinetic CP: trial warranted, less dramatic.

MANDATORY trial in any child with dystonia and normal MRI — DRD (GCH1) is exquisitely responsive and clinically indistinguishable from dyskinetic CP without a trial. Low risk. Absence of response does not exclude DRD definitively (consider CSF neurotransmitter testing).

Nausea (give with food); dyskinesia at higher doses (rare in DRD); hypotension; very rarely: dopamine dysregulation (high doses).

Clonazepam

GABA-A positive modulator (same as diazepam but longer-acting)

Dystonia; myoclonus; mixed tone disorders

0.01–0.05 mg/kg/day divided BID-TID; titrate slowly

Useful adjunct for dystonia, especially at night. Helpful for dystonic spasms and sleep-disrupting tone abnormalities. Myoclonus-dystonia overlap.

Sedation; tolerance; hypersalivation (problematic in CP with dysphagia). Paradoxical agitation possible.

Tetrabenazine / deutetrabenazine

VMAT2 inhibitor → depletes presynaptic dopamine and monoamines

Hyperkinetic movements: choreoathetosis, dystonia (variable), tics

Tetrabenazine: start 12.5 mg/day; titrate. Deutetrabenazine (longer half-life): start 6 mg/day.

More commonly used for chorea and choreoathetosis (Huntington, neuroacanthocytosis, dyskinetic CP with prominent chorea). Variable benefit for dystonia. Deutetrabenazine has improved tolerability profile.

Depression/suicidality (screen; contraindicated in untreated depression); sedation; Parkinsonism (dose-dependent); QTc prolongation.

FOCAL AND INTERVENTIONAL TREATMENTS

Botulinum toxin A (BoNT-A) (onabotulinumtoxinA / abobotulinumtoxinA)

Blocks presynaptic ACh release at NMJ → focal chemodenervation → temporary reduction of muscle overactivity

Focal spasticity; focal dystonia

Dose per muscle group; total dose limits apply (onabotulinumtoxinA: ≤12–16 U/kg per session; abobotulinumtoxinA: ~3× conversion). IM injection, often with ultrasound guidance or EMG.

Most commonly used focal treatment in CP. Most evidence for equinus (gastrocnemius/soleus), scissoring (hip adductors), and UE spasticity. Effects last 3–6 months — repeat every 3–4 months with therapy to consolidate gains. Combine with casting and physiotherapy for optimal outcomes. Dystonia: less evidence but used for focal dystonia (writer's cramp, cervical dystonia analogs in CP).

Injection site pain; weakness (excessive spread); dysphagia if neck muscles treated; very rare systemic spread (FDA black box — distant site weakness). Antibody formation with high-dose, high-frequency use (less common with onabotulinumtoxinA if intervals maintained).

Intrathecal baclofen (ITB pump)

Direct delivery of baclofen to CSF → high spinal concentration, minimal systemic exposure → potent reduction of spinal spasticity and some dystonia

Severe spasticity (especially LE); dystonia (variable response); mixed tone

Programmable pump implanted in abdominal wall; catheter to lumbar intrathecal space. Starting dose ~25–50 mcg/day; titrate over months. Bolus screening trial before implant.

Indicated for severe, diffuse spasticity at GMFCS III–V not adequately managed with oral agents or BoNT. Significantly reduces tone, improves care (hygiene, positioning), reduces pain. Dystonic CP response is variable — less predictable than for spasticity. Goal may be comfort and care, not ambulation.

PUMP/CATHETER MALFUNCTION → acute baclofen withdrawal (fever, altered mental status, seizures, autonomic instability — MEDICAL EMERGENCY). Infection (pump pocket, meningitis). Overdose → coma, respiratory arrest. Residents must recognize withdrawal syndrome urgently.

Selective dorsal rhizotomy (SDR)

Surgical sectioning of selective dorsal nerve rootlets (L1–S1) → permanently reduces afferent spasticity drive

Spasticity (permanent reduction — not dystonia)

Surgical procedure; not reversible

Best evidence for spastic diplegia at GMFCS II–III with good underlying muscle strength and minimal dystonia. Requires careful patient selection. Permanent reduction in LE spasticity. Combined with intensive post-operative physiotherapy. Does not benefit dystonia — misidentification of dystonia as spasticity leads to poor outcomes.

Permanent (irreversible); post-operative weakness; bladder dysfunction (usually transient); spinal deformity risk long-term. Patient selection is critical — outcomes poor if significant dystonia component present.

Baclofen Withdrawal — A Medical Emergency Residents Must Recognize

  • Acute baclofen withdrawal (oral or intrathecal pump failure) presents with: fever, altered mental status, severe spasticity/rigidity, seizures, autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis).
  • It can be fatal if not recognized and treated promptly.
  • In ITB pump failure: the pump may alarm, or the catheter may kink/migrate. Do not assume 'the pump is fine' without interrogating it.
  • Treatment: high-dose oral or IV benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam) bridge the gap while the pump is repaired. Oral baclofen does not achieve adequate spinal concentrations to substitute for ITB in severe cases — benzodiazepines are the acute treatment.
  • Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) has been used as adjunctive treatment for ITB withdrawal but evidence is limited.
  • Any child with an ITB pump presenting with fever + altered mental status = baclofen withdrawal until proven otherwise. Call neurosurgery.

Levodopa Trial — Mandatory in Dystonia with Normal MRI

  • Dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD) is clinically indistinguishable from dyskinetic CP without a levodopa trial.
  • Protocol: levodopa/carbidopa, starting at ~1 mg/kg/day levodopa component, divided TID, titrated over 2–4 weeks to 2–5 mg/kg/day.
  • DRD response: dramatic improvement, often within days to a few weeks. Diurnal variation disappears. Function may normalize remarkably.
  • Absence of dramatic response does not definitively exclude DRD — some cases respond only partially, or have the recessive TH gene form (requires higher doses). CSF neurotransmitters (low HVA, low biopterin) can support the diagnosis.
  • The levodopa trial is low-risk, inexpensive, and potentially life-changing. There is no acceptable reason not to perform it in a child with dystonia and a normal MRI.
